coredata.dc:description: Water pollution induced by the productive process of organic dyes would lead to various diseases, and so treatment of the wastewater is an urgent task for protecting human health and ecological environment. In this work, visible light responsive heterojunction material based on silver iodide and carbon dots (CDs) doped ZIF-8 metal organic frameworks (CDs@ZIF-8@AgI) has been prepared by a green solvent-free strategy and in-situ ion deposition method. The CDs@ZIF-8@AgI composites have high photocatalytic performance for degradation of acridine orange (AO) dye under visible light, and the maximum degradation percentage can reach to 96.0%, which was estimated by the UV–visible spectroscopy. The porous structure and large surface area of the photocatalyst were favorable to absorption of the target dye on the catalyst, which can promote the kinetic degradation rate for low level concentration of the dye. The introduction of CDs into the AgI heterojunction can improve the electron transport efficiency and visible light absorption capacity, and thus significantly enhancing the photocatalytic activity of the catalyst. The photocatalytic mechanism was proposed as indirect Z-scheme heterojunction type based on the energy band structure of the catalyst. This work provides a visible light photocatalyst for removal of the AO dye in wastewater and offers a promising strategy for developing photocatalyst for treatment of organic pollutants in the environment using solar energy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8, AgI, ZIF-8@AgI and CDs@ZIF-8@AgI; (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@ZIF-8 under various excitation wavelengths ranged from 360 nm to 480 nm; (c) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8 under excitation wavelength of 480 nm; (d) FT-IR spectra of ZIF-8, ZIF-8@AgI, CDs@ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8@AgI.
SEM images of (a) CDs@ZIF-8 and (b) CDs@ZIF-8@AgI; TEM images of (c) CDs@ZIF-8, and (d) CDs@ZIF-8@AgI; HRTEM images of (e) CDs@ZIF-8 and (f) CDs@ZIF-8@AgI.

(a) XPS spectra of AgI, CDs@ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8@AgI; XPS spectra of CDs@ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8@AgI for (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, (d) O 1 s and (e) Zn 2p, respectively; XPS spectra of AgI and CDs@ZIF-8@AgI for (f) Ag 3d, (g) I 3d, respectively; the fitting XPS C 1 s spectra of (h) CDs@ZIF-8 and (i) ZIF-8.

(a) The degradation curves of AO between C/C0 and reaction time when catalyzed by CDs@ZIF-8@AgI, ZIF-8@AgI, CDs@ZIF-8, ZIF-8 and AgI under visible light (λ > 420 nm), respectively; (b) The degradation curves between C/C0 and reaction time for various initial concentration of AO ranged from 5.00 μg/mL to 25.0 μg/mL when catalyzed by CDs@ZIF-8@AgI under visible light (λ > 420 nm), respectively; (c) the kinetic regression curves of -ln(C/C0) versus time of degradation reaction for the data of (b); (d) the degradation percentages of BB, MO, RhB, CIP, CTC and AO when catalyzed by CDs@ZIF-8@AgI under visible light (λ > 420 nm) for 90 min.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of AO at various pH ranged from 4.0 to 10.0, and pH 7.0′ and pH 7.0′' represented that the pH value of 9.0 or 10.0 was adjusted to pH 7.0, respectively; (b) The catalytic degradation performance of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI for AO (20.0 μg/mL) in various pH conditions ranged from 4.0 to 10.0; (c) The catalytic degradation performance of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI for AO (15.0 μg/mL) in various dosage of the catalyst ranged from 0.100 mg/mL to 0.700 mg/mL; (d) The catalytic degradation performance of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI for AO (15.0 μg/mL) in the existence of various co-existing ions, and the concentration of Mg2+ and Ca2+ was 1.0 mmol·L-1 and that of NO3 –, SO4 2- and Cl- was 2.5 mmol·L-1, respectively.

(a) The effect of scavengers on the catalytic degradation performance of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI for AO dye (15.0 μg/mL) under visible light (λ > 420 nm), when the concentration of scavenger (TEMPO, EDTA-2Na and IPA) was 0.100 mol/L, respectively; (b) Photocurrent responses of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI, ZIF-8@AgI, AgI, CDs@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8 for eight cycles; (c) The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI, ZIF-8@AgI, AgI, CDs@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8 particles; (d) Plots of (F(R)hν)2 versus photo energy (hν) of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI, AgI and CDs@ZIF-8; The Mott-Schottky curves of (e) CDs@ZIF-8 and (f) AgI.
Possible photocatalytic mechanisms of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI for degradation of AO dye: (a) the typical type-II heterojunction; (b) the indirect Z-scheme heterojunction.
Schematic illustration for the synthetic procedures of CDs@ZIF-8-AgI.

head.ce:title: Visible light assisted heterojunction composite of AgI and CDs doped ZIF-8 metal-organic framework for photocatalytic degradation of organic dye

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical and Testing Center of Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The two authors have equal contribution to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water pollution induced by the productive process of organic dyes would lead to various diseases, and so treatment of the wastewater is an urgent task for protecting human health and ecological environment. In this work, visible light responsive heterojunction material based on silver iodide and carbon dots (CDs) doped ZIF-8 metal organic frameworks (CDs@ZIF-8@AgI) has been prepared by a green solvent-free strategy and in-situ ion deposition method. The CDs@ZIF-8@AgI composites have high photocatalytic performance for degradation of acridine orange (AO) dye under visible light, and the maximum degradation percentage can reach to 96.0%, which was estimated by the UV–visible spectroscopy. The porous structure and large surface area of the photocatalyst were favorable to absorption of the target dye on the catalyst, which can promote the kinetic degradation rate for low level concentration of the dye. The introduction of CDs into the AgI heterojunction can improve the electron transport efficiency and visible light absorption capacity, and thus significantly enhancing the photocatalytic activity of the catalyst. The photocatalytic mechanism was proposed as indirect Z-scheme heterojunction type based on the energy band structure of the catalyst. This work provides a visible light photocatalyst for removal of the AO dye in wastewater and offers a promising strategy for developing photocatalyst for treatment of organic pollutants in the environment using solar energy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic dyes are widely utilized in the industry of textile, leather, paper making and cosmetics [1], while the extensive use of dyes would unavoidably bring a large number of polluted effluents. Nowadays, the total annual production of synthetic dyes in global was over a million tons [2], and>15 % of the colorants were discharged into the water environment, which contained highly hazardous and non-biodegradable dye components [3]. The dye pollutants can cause serious damage to human health, leading to many diseases, such as irritation, intoxication, mutation or cancer [4]. Acridine orange (AO) is a nitrogen containing heterocyclic cationic dye and is mainly utilized in printing and biological staining [5]. It is irritating and harmful if inhaled or contacted, and previous investigations have reported that the AO dye may lead allergic reaction and skin damage [6], and cause mutation due to the mutagenic potential of amino acridine [7]. Therefore, the degradation and disposal of residual AO dye in wastewater is an urgent need to ensure the human health.

A variety of methods have been utilized to treat the dye wastewater [8,9], mainly including biological and physical technologies. However, biological methods required long processing period and were highly sensitive to external conditions [10]. Physical methods, such as adsorption, precipitation and coagulation, can only achieve the transformation of pollutants from liquid phase to solid phase, which were easy to cause secondary pollution [11]. Recent years, the emerging photocatalytic technology for treatment of wastewater has drawn much attention of the research community, and the photocatalysts have the ability to convert solar energy to chemical energy, and generate high oxidation potential of reactive oxygen species, which can degrade toxic organic dyes to harmless substances [8,12–13].

Traditional photocatalysts, such as TiO2 and ZnO, can only absorb ultra violet light due to their wide energy band gap, thus limited their practical application [14,15]. Recent years, the research focus has been paid to develop novel photocatalytic materials with wide light absorption range, which were mainly complex materials based on metal oxide [16], semiconductor [17], metal–organic framework [18] or other heterojunction composites with excellent photoelectric properties [19]. Silver iodide (AgI) particles have negative conduction band potential, and so they can promote the generation of reactive oxygen species under irradiation of visible light [20]. Hence, AgI based nanomaterials have been intensively concerned, and various photocatalysts containing AgI component have been developed for catalytic degradation of pollutants [21–23]. For instance, silver iodide/graphitic carbon nitride nanocomposites were prepared and utilized for the catalytic degradation of rhodamine B and methyl orange under visible light [24]. The formation of AgI/Sb2O3 heterojunction can be used as a photocatalyst to degrade Acid Red G [25]. However, some effects are still needed to develop AgI-based photocatalysts for promoting the visible light absorption and improving catalytic activity.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a class of porous crystalline organic–inorganic materials with large surface area, devisable structure, tunable porosity and semiconductor property, which have potential applications in adsorption/separation, catalysis and carriers of establishing complex materials [26–28]. Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks-8 (ZIF-8) is a typical MOFs prepared by Zn2+ and imidazolate organic ligands, which possesses both the excellent stability of zeolite and the structural characteristic of MOFs [29]. Hence, ZIF-8 can be combined with other materials to construct novel photocatalyst [30,31]. Carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging type of carbon nanomaterials, have excellent photoelectric performance, high chemical stability and abundant preparation methods [32,33]. Introduction of CDs into the photocatalysts can improve the visible light absorption and promote the separation of photoinduced electron/holes in the processes of photocatalysis [34–36]. The ZIF-8 MOFs are ideal carriers for CDs due to their large surface areas and cavities [37,38].

This work aims to develop a heterojunction material (CDs@ZIF-8@AgI) based on the AgI and CDs doped ZIF-8 MOFs with porous structure, which acts as a photocatalyst for the degradation of acridine orange (AO) under visible light. The photocatalytic performance of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI has been assessed and the effect of some factors on the degradation of AO has been estimated. The possible mechanisms of the photocatalytic degradation were proposed based on the energy band structure of the photocatalyst and active species of the degradation system. The results showed that the photocatalyst CDs@ZIF-8@AgI can rapidly degrade the dye of AO under visible light with high efficiency, which provided new ideas and approaches for developing photocatalyst for removal of pollutants in the environment.

Zinc oxide (ZnO, AR), 2-methylimidazole (2-MIM, 98.0 %), silver nitrate (AgNO3, AR) and potassium iodide (KI, AR) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., ltd (Shanghai, China). Acridine orange (AO, AR), methyl orange (MO, AR), rhodamine B (RhB, AR) and brilliant blue (BB, AR) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Saint Louis, USA). Ciprofloxacin (CIP, 98 %) was from Beijing J&K Scientific Co., ltd (Beijing, China). Chlortetracycline hydrochloride (CTC, AR) was from Sangon Biotech Co., ltd (Shanghai, China). 2,2,6,6-tetra-methylpiperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO, 98.0 %) was from China Macklin Chemical Reagent Co., ltd (Shanghai, China). Ethanol (EtOH), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), anhydrous zinc acetate (Zn (CH3COO)2), nitric acid (HNO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), sodium nitrate (NaNO3) and other reagents were all analytical grade and from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). Deionized water was obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q apparatus (Bedford, USA).

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) of the samples were collected by using a PANalytical X′Pert PRO X-ray diffractometer (Eindhoven, Holland) with a Cu anode material. The morphology of samples was characterized by using Hitachi S-4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Tokyo, Japan) with an acceleration voltage of 20 kV accompanied with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) of samples were obtained by a FEI Tecnai G2 f 20 s- twin 200 kV field emission transmission electron microscopy (Hillsboro, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed on a Thermo-Fisher ESCALAB 250 Xi(Massachusetts, USA). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Horiba JY FluoroMax-4 spectrophotometer (Paris, France). The infrared spectra of samples were determined on a Thermo-Fisher Nicolet IS 50 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Madison, USA) with an accumulation of 64 scans and a resolution of 4 cm−1 in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms were recorded by a Quantachrome Quadrasorb SI surface area and pore size analyzer (Florida, USA). The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of samples were performed on a PerkinElmer LAMBDA 950 ultraviolet spectrophotometer (Massachusetts, USA) equipped with an integrating sphere attachment and a BaSO4 reference within the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The Zeta-potential was recorded on a Brookhaven Zeta Plus analyzer (New York, USA). The UV–Visible absorption spectra of the target components were determined by a Thermo-Fisher Varioskan LUX automatic microplate reader (Waltham, USA). Transient photocurrents (I-t), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Mott-Schottky (M−S) curves were tested on the Shanghai Chenhua CHI 660E electrochemical station (Shanghai, China) with a standard three-electrode system. The analysis of AO dye and its degradation products were performed by an Aglient 1260 High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) coupled with a Bruker microTOF-QII tandem quadrupole time-of-flight mass spectrometer (Q-TOF-MS) (Massachusetts, USA).

A CY-GHX-AC Photochemical reaction apparatus equipped with xenon lamp (power of 500 W with a UV cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm)) and cryostat (DC-0510) was utilized for photocatalytic degradation of the target components (Shanghai Julai Experimental Instrument, Co., ltd. Shanghai, China).

CDs@ZIF-8 particles were prepared by a solvent-free method according to previous report with some modifications [37]. Briefly, ZnO (81.4 mg) and 2-MIM (411 mg) were mixed and grinded in a mortar for 10 min. Then, the mixture was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon stainless steel reactor and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the products (CDs@ZIF-8) were washed with ethanol for three times and separated by centrifugation (7000 rpm, 10 min), and then dried at 60 °C for 8 h. Meanwhile, CDs@ZIF-8 with different molecular ratios of ZnO and 2-MIM (1:3, 1:4 and 1:6) was synthesized in the same procedures, respectively.

CDs@ZIF-8@AgI composite was prepared by an in-situ ion deposition of AgI on the surface of CDs@ZIF-8. 160 mg of CDs@ZIF-8 was dispersed into 35 mL deionized water with ultrasonic for 40 min. Then, 49.8 mg of KI was added into the solution and stirred for 1 h. After that, 1.48 mL of 0.20 mol/L AgNO3 aqueous solution was dropped into the suspension and kept stirring intensely for 12 h in darkness. Thereafter, precipitation was washed with deionized water for three times and separated by centrifugation (7000 rpm, 10 min). Finally, the product was dried at 40 °C for 8 h.

CDs@ZIF-8@AgI composites with various weight ratios of AgI and CDs@ZIF-8 were prepared with the same procedures as described above, respectively. Furthermore, AgI nanoparticles were prepared in dark by a simple precipitation reaction of KI and AgNO3 aqueous solutions at room temperature.

ZIF-8 nanocrystals were synthesized following our previously reported method [39] with the molecular ratio of 25 between zinc acetate and 2-methylimidazole. The procedures for preparation of ZIF-8@AgI were same with those of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI.

The photocatalyst CDs@ZIF-8@AgI (15.0 mg) was added to 30.0 mL aqueous solution of AO (15.0 μg/mL), and the mixture solution was stirred for 30 min under dark condition to achieve an adsorption equilibrium and then subjected to the catalytic reaction under irradiation of visible light for photocatalytic degradation of AO for another 40 min. The reaction solution (600 μL) was drawn from the system at certain time intervals and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 3.0 min to remove the photocatalyst particles completely. Then, concentration of AO in the reaction solution of each time intervals was determined by the intensity of the absorbance at 491 nm (method S1), respectively.

The photocatalytic performances of other materials (CDs@ZIF-8, AgI, ZIF-8 and ZIF-8@ AgI) for absorption and degradation of AO (15.0 μg/mL) were investigated in the same procedures and conditions, respectively. The photocatalytic performances of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI for different AO concentrations (5.00 μg/mL, 10.0 μg/mL, 15.0 μg/mL, 20.0 μg/mL and 25.0 μg/mL) were performed in the same procedures and conditions, respectively.

The photocatalytic performance of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI for other pollutants (BB, MO, RhB, CIP and CTC) were performed in the same conditions, respectively. The concentration of each pollutant was 15.0 μg/mL, and the reaction time was 90 min. The remaining concentration of each pollutant in the reaction system was estimated by the UV–vis absorbance before and after the degradation reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence factors for catalytic activity of catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectra of AO aqueous solution (15.0 μg/mL) at various pH conditions ranged from 4.0 to 10.0 were measured with a microplate analyzer, and the pH value of AO solution was adjusted by HNO3 (0.2 mol/L) or NaOH (0.2 mol/L) solution.

CDs@ZIF-8@AgI (15.0 mg) was added to 30.0 mL of AO (20.0 μg/mL) aqueous solution (pH 7.0), and the adsorption and photocatalytic degradation reaction were performed as the procedures described in section 2.4. Similarly, the pH value of AO solution ranged from 4.0 to 10.0 can be adjusted by HNO3 (0.2 mol/L) or NaOH (0.2 mol/L) solution, respectively, to investigate the influence of pH on the catalytic performance of the catalyst for degradation of AO.

For investigating the effects of the catalyst dosage on the catalytic degradation of AO, various amounts of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI (3.0 mg, 9.0 mg, 15.0 mg and 21.0 mg) were added to 30.0 mL of AO (15.0 μg/mL) aqueous solution (pH 7.0), respectively. The adsorption and photocatalytic degradation reaction were performed as the procedures described in section 2.4.

To examine the effect of co-existing ions, 1.0 mmol·L-1 magnesium nitrate, calcium nitrate and 2.5 mmol·L-1 sodium nitrate, sodium chloride and sodium sulfate were added to the reaction system, respectively. The adsorption and photocatalytic degradation reaction were performed as the procedures described in section 2.4.

To determine the active reactive species, 0.100 mol/L of scavengers (TEMPO, EDTA-2Na, and IPA) were introduced to the photocatalytic degradation reaction system, respectively. The other procedures were same to those of section 2.4.

Scheme 1 shows the strategy for preparation of the photocatalyst CDs@ZIF-8@AgI. The procedure was started with an one-pot synthetic approach without solvent [37], which mixed ZnO and 2-MIM together by a simple solid-state grinding method combined with a subsequent thermal-heating treatment to obtain CDs@ZIF-8. The excess organic ligand (2-MIM) would be partially carbonized into CDs and encapsulated into ZIF-8 MOFs during the heating crystallization process. Next, an in-situ ion deposition approach under room temperature was utilized to prepare the heterojunction composite. The I− ions can be adsorbed onto the surface of CDs@ZIF-8 due to the interaction of I− and Zn2+ of the ZIF-8 MOFs, and then, with addition of AgNO3, the AgI can be in-situ deposited on the surface of CDs@ZIF-8, forming the heterojunction photocatalyst of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI.

The guest CDs in the photocatalyst can optimize the optical transmission properties of the subject ZIF-8 due to their well charge transportation ability [40], leading to enhancement of the photocatalytic activity. The uniform framework of ZIF-8 avoids the aggregation of CDs and ensures the synergetic effect of CDs and ZIF-8 to promote the electron transfer. The formation of heterojunction with AgI further improves its absorption capacity of visible light and separation efficiency for the photogenerated electron hole pairs [41], which has potential application in the photocatalytic degradation of environmental pollutants.

To optimize the preparation conditions, the effects of the feed ratios (ZnO, 2-MIM and AgI) on the photocatalytic performance of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI for degradation of the AO dye under visible light were investigated. Fixing the content of AgI, when the molecular ratios of ZnO and 2-MIM were changed from 1:3 to 1:5, the photocatalytic degradation percentages of AO augmented from 73.0 % to 96.0 % (Table S1). With further raising the molecular ratio of ZnO and 2-MIM to 1:6, the degradation percentage of AO slightly reduced to 88.1 %. Meanwhile, the effects of the weight ratio for AgI on the photocatalytic performance of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI were investigated under fixing the molecular ratio of ZnO and 2-MIM to 1:5. As the data in Table S1, the degradation percentages of AO were 54.1 %, 96.0 % and 81.8 % when the weight ratios of AgI were 18 %, 30 % and 47 %, respectively. The results illustrated that the photocatalyst has the highest catalytic activity for degradation of the AO dye when the molecular ratio of ZnO and 2-MIM was 1:5 and the weight ratio of AgI in the catalyst was 30 %.

The powder XRD pattern of the CDs@ZIF-8 was same with that of ZIF-8 prepared by in-situ coprecipitation method of our previous works [42] (Fig. S1 a), and no obvious peaks of CDs were observed because of its small sizes or the low content [43]. The XRD pattern of AgI crystal shows that the four main characteristic peaks are at 23.83°, 32.13°, 39.39° and 46.23°, corresponding to the (002), (102), (110) and (112) crystal planes (JCPDS No. 09–0374) [44], respectively. In the XRD pattern of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI, the main characteristic peaks of CDs@ZIF-8 and AgI can be clearly seen, which illustrated that the heterojunction composites were successfully synthesized.

With changing the molecular ratios of ZnO and 2-MIM from 1:3 to 1:6, the main characteristic peaks for the XRD pattern of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI were nearly same (Fig. S1b), indicating that all the composites have high crystallinity [37]. However, with increasing the content of AgI in the CDs@ZIF-8@AgI, the relative intensities of the ZIF-8 characteristic peaks were decreased (Fig. S1c), which may be originated that the coverage of AgI on the surface of CDs@ZIF-8 would slightly reduce the crystallinity of the ZIF-8 main structure [45]. In the following, CDs@ZIF-8-c and CDs@ZIF-8-c@AgI-2 were denoted as CDs@ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8@AgI, respectively.

Fig. 1a shows the UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra of ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8, AgI, ZIF-8@AgI and CDs@ZIF-8@AgI. There was only a very weak absorption band above 250 nm for ZIF-8, which approximately centered at 300 nm [46], while a strong and broad adsorption band (approximately centered at 320 nm) between 250 nm and 800 nm can be observed for CDs@ZIF-8, which indicated that the CDs has been formed and encapsulated in the skeleton of ZIF-8 [47]. From the spectrum of AgI, it can be seen that there was a weak broad band between 500 nm and 800 nm apart from the strong band below 500 nm, which illustrated that AgI can adsorb the visible light. When the AgI was deposited on the CDs@ZIF-8, the absorbance intensity of the band between 500 nm and 800 nm was obviously enhanced, indicating that its ability to adsorb the visible light was improved [48]. Furthermore, the absorbance intensity of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI was significantly higher than that of ZIF-8@AgI, signifying that introduction of CDs on the heterojunction composite can effectively augment its ability of absorbing visible light.

Carbon dots have typical feature of tunable fluorescence (FL) arising from the quantum confinement effect [49], and so the FL property of the CDs@ZIF-8 has been investigated to verify the formation of the carbon dots. As shown in Fig. 1b and Table S2, the FL maximum emission wavelengths of CDs@ZIF-8 were gradually red-shifted from 468 nm to 547 nm when the excitation wavelengths were raised from 360 nm to 500 nm. Meanwhile, the ZIF-8 exhibited no fluorescence emission when the excitation wavelength was 480 nm (Fig. 1c). The red-shift phenomenon of the FL maximum emission wavelengths reflected the existence of CDs in the composite [47].

Fig. 1d shows the Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra of ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8, ZIF-8@AgI and CDs@ZIF-8@AgI. For ZIF-8, the characteristic bands at 3133 cm−1 and 2929 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of the NH and CH bond for the organic ligand, respectively [50]. The band at 1583 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the CN bond, while the strong bands at 1456 cm−1 and 1422 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of the imidazole ring [27]. The bands in the range of 1175–950 cm−1 and the bands at 754 and 691 cm−1 were corresponding to the in-plane bending vibrations of the imidazole ring and the CH bending vibrations, and the band at 420 cm−1 belonged to the Zn-N stretching [51]. The main FTIR bands of CDs@ZIF-8, ZIF-8@AgI and CDs@ZIF-8@AgI were nearly same with those of ZIF-8 apart from the weak band at about 1700 cm−1 for CDs@ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8@AgI, which came from the stretching vibration of the CO bond in CDs [47]. The results illustrated that the deposition of AgI and encapsulation of the CDs have little effect on the structure of the ZIF-8.

The morphology of CDs@ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8@AgI was observed by scan electron microscopy (SEM) approach (Fig. 2 a and b). The profile of CDs@ZIF-8 composite presented as irregular polyhedron crystal particles with particle size of approximately 1.0 µm, and the surface of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI became much rougher when the AgI nanoparticles were in-situ deposited (Fig. 2b). Furthermore, the SEM-EDS elemental mapping images of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI showed the equal distribution of N, C, O, Zn, Ag and I in the composite (Fig. S2). Among them, the existence of oxygen element may be originated from carbon dots.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CDs@ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8@AgI were shown in Fig. 2c and 2d. It can be seen that the profile of CDs@ZIF-8 composite was obvious polyhedral cubic crystal (Fig. 2c), and the AgI nanoparticles were anchored on the surface of CDs@ZIF-8. From the high-resolution TEM images of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI, some dot particles were observed in the crystal structure with particle size of approximately 1.8 nm, suggesting the existence of CDs particles (Fig. 2e). Besides, it can be clearly observed that the lattice spacing of AgI nanoparticles on the surface of CDs@ZIF-8 was 0.230 nm (Fig. 2f), which can be assigned to the (110) plane of AgI [41].

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CDs@ZIF-8, AgI and CDs@ZIF-8@AgI was determined (Fig. 3 ). It can be seen that the CDs@ZIF-8 was mainly composed of C, N, O, and Zn elements, and CDs@ZIF-8@AgI contained the chemical compositions of C, N, O, Zn, Ag and I (Fig. 3a). The C 1 s and N 1 s spectra showed that their binding energy had no obvious difference between CDs@ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8@AgI (Fig. 3b and 3c). As shown in Fig. 3d, the characteristic peak of O 1 s for CDs@ZIF-8@AgI was shifted to higher binding energy (531.6 eV) comparing to that of CDs@ZIF-8 (531.2 eV). Similarly, from the characteristic peaks in Zn 2p spectra (Fig. 3e), it can be seen that the binding energy of Zn2+ [52] for CDs@ZIF-8 (1021.7 eV and 1044.8 eV) was also shifted to the higher binding energy (1021.9 eV and 1045.1 eV) for CDs@ZIF-8@AgI. However, the binding energy of Ag 3d at 368.5 eV and 374.5 eV, which corresponded to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 for AgI, shifted to lower binding energy of 368.3 eV and 374.2 eV (Fig. 3f) for CDs@ZIF-8@AgI. Meanwhile, the binding energy of I 3d shifted from 619.7 eV and 631.3 eV to 619.4 eV and 631.1 eV (Fig. 3g), which were ascribed to I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2 [44,48], after formation of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI. The red- or blue-shift of the binding energy for the compositions of the hybrid material can be regarded as the evidence for the formation of intimate interfacial contact between CDs@ZIF-8 and AgI [53].

Furthermore, the C1s spectrum of CDs@ZIF-8 can be decomposed into four peaks (Fig. 3h), corresponding to CC, C–sp3, CN and CO with binding energy of 284.1 eV, 284.9 eV, 285.9 eV and 287.3 eV, respectively [47]. Meanwhile, C1s spectrum of ZIF-8 was only decomposed into three peaks (Fig. 3i), belonging to CC, C–sp3 and CN of the organic ligand [54]. The extra fitting peak at 287.3 eV may be ascribed to the CO group of the CDs encapsulated in the ZIF-8 nanocrystal [37].

Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI and ZIF-8@AgI were measured to examine the BET surface area and pore size distribution (Fig. S3). The isotherms of both CDs@ZIF-8@AgI and ZIF-8@AgI exhibited a typical type I curve [55], indicating the microporous and narrow mesoporous characteristics, and the BET surface areas of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI and ZIF-8@AgI were 1165.4 m2/g and 1027.1 m2/g, respectively. The pore size distribution was calculated by Barret-Joyner-Halenda method [56], and it was mainly between 2.27 and 4.28 nm for CDs@ZIF-8@AgI, while it was primarily 1.24 nm for ZIF-8@AgI. The foregoing results of characterization demonstrated that the heterojunction material, CDs@ZIF-8@AgI, has been formed, and it has porous structure and large surface area.

The adsorption property and photocatalytic degradation performance of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI for the AO dye were investigated under dark and irradiation of visible light (λ > 420 nm), respectively, and compared with those of ZIF-8@AgI, CDs@ZIF-8, ZIF-8 and AgI (Fig. 4 a). The adsorption capacity of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI in dark can reach to 25.1 % of its original concentration after 30 min and then remained nearly constant with prolonging the time to 60 min (Fig.S4), which was similar to that of CDs@ZIF-8 and obviously higher than those of ZIF-8@AgI, ZIF-8 and AgI, respectively. The higher adsorption capacity of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI may be originated from the porous structure and large surface area of the catalyst, which was favorable to promote the catalytic performance of AO [57].

Without the photocatalyst, the concentration of AO was only reduced approximately 3 % after irradiation with visible light for 40 min (Fig. 4a), indicating that the self-degradation of AO was slight. When catalyzed by CDs@ZIF-8@AgI, the remaining percentages of AO in the degradation system rapidly decreased with prolonging the time of irradiation, and the degradation efficiency of AO can reach to 96.0 % after 40 min. In the similar conditions, the degradation efficiency of AO catalyzed by ZIF-8@AgI and AgI was 73.0 % and 45.1 %, and that by ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 was 17.9 % and 33.1 %, respectively. The results illustrated that the CDs@ZIF-8@AgI had effective degradation performance for the AO dye, and the AgI deposited on the CDs@ZIF-8 played a key role to improve the catalytic activity of the heterojunction material due to the enhancement of visible light response [20]. Furthermore, introduction of the CDs can increase the electron transport and optical absorption properties, thus enhanced the catalytic degradation ability of ZIF-8 based photocatalyst [58].

The UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra of AO during photocatalytic degradation were collected (Fig.S5). The intensity of the UV–vis maximum absorption band (λmax = 491 nm) of AO was gradually decreased with increasing irradiation time of visible light from 0 min to 40 min (Fig. S5 a). Similarly, the fluorescence intensity (530 nm) of AO with excitation of Ex = 491 nm behaved the same manner (Fig. S5b).

The adsorption and degradation performance of the photocatalyst for various concentrations of AO ranged from 5.00 μg/mL to 25.0 μg/mL has been estimated (Fig. 4b). The remaining percentages of AO after incubation with the catalyst for 30 min were gradually reduced when the initial concentration of AO was decreased from 25.0 μg/mL to 5.00 μg/mL, and it can reach to 33.2 % when the concentration of AO was 5.00 μg/mL. The results indicated that the photocatalyst had good adsorption ability for the target dye, and it can act as an adsorbent to enrich the low concentration level of the dye, which was beneficial to improve its catalytic degradation efficiency [59].

As shown in Fig. 4b, the remaining percentages of AO in the catalytic degradation system were gradually decreased with prolonging the reaction time for all concentration levels. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency for 40 min increased from 76.8 % to 96.0 % when the initial dye concentrations were from 25.0 μg/mL to 15.0 μg/mL, and then nearly remained constant (95.1 % and 93.0 %) in lower concentration levels (10.0 μg/mL to 5.00 μg/mL).

In order to better understand the catalytic degradation behaviors of AO in various concentration levels, the kinetics (zero-order, first-order and second-order) of the degradation reaction have been investigated [8]. When the concentration of AO was 25.0 μg/mL, 20.0 μg/mL and 15.0 μg/mL, the linear regression curves presented good linearity between ln (C0/C) and the time of degradation reaction ranged from 0.0 min to 40.0 min (Fig. 4c), which indicated that the catalytic degradation reaction followed the pseudo-first-order kinetics model [8,60]. Therefore, the photocatalytic kinetic degradation rate constants (DRC) were estimated by the slopes of the linear regression equation, which was 3.19 × 10-2 min−1, 4.16 × 10-2 min−1 and 7.21 × 10-2 min−1 for 25.0 μg/mL, 20.0 μg/mL and 15.0 μg/mL, respectively. In lower concentration levels (10.0 μg/mL and 5.00 μg/mL), the photocatalytic degradation reaction of AO was fast in the initial stage, and so their linear regression equation between ln (C0/C) and time of degradation reaction was obtained in the linear range, and their DRCs were 8.98 × 10-2 min−1 and 10.1 × 10-2 min−1 for 10.0 μg/mL and 5.00 μg/mL, respectively. The results illustrated that the photocatalytic degradation rate augmented with decreasing the initial concentration of AO in the system, which inferred that the photocatalytic degradation mainly occurred in the surface of the catalyst. For the lower concentration levels of AO (10.0 μg/mL and 5.00 μg/mL), the adsorption percentages on the catalyst were relatively high, and so their degradation rates were initially fast and the degradation reaction equilibrium was rapidly achieved in 20 min and 10 min, respectively.

The photocatalytic degradation performance of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI for other organic dyes (brilliant blue (BB), methyl orange (MO), rhodamine B (RhB)) and antibiotics (ciprofloxacin (CIP), chlorotetracycline (CTC)) was tried under the irradiation of visible light for 90 min (Fig. 4d). It has been found that the degradation percentages were 60.8 %, 54.5 %, 53.1 % for the organic dyes (BB, MO and RhB), and 76.5 % and 80.2 % for the antibiotics (CIP and CTC), respectively. The results illustrated that the photocatalyst had potential applications for degrading various pollutants, and the variations of the removal rates may be caused by the differences of the stability for these organic molecules [61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence factors for the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of AO
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–visible absorption spectra of AO would be affected by the pH of the solution. When the pH value was above 9.0, the maximum absorption band of AO was blue shifted from 491 nm to 433 nm accompanying obvious reduction of the absorption intensity (Fig. 5a). However, when the pH of AO solution was adjusted from 9.0 (or 10.0) to 7.0, the maximum absorption band of AO was recovered to 491 nm (Fig. 5a). The pKa value of AO is 9.8 and the increase of alkaline would lead to the deprotonation of AO (Fig. S6 a), thus the energy interval between the ground state and excited state of AO molecule would be changed [62]. Meanwhile, the AO solutions in pH = 7.0 and 9.0 were determined by mass spectrometry, respectively, and the results showed that they have the same mass spectra (Fig. S6b and 6c), indicating that the change of pH did not cause the self-degradation of AO.

The effects of pH values on the catalytic performance of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI for degradation of AO (20.0 μg/mL) were investigated (Fig. 5b). The catalytic degradation percentages of AO for 40 min increased from 44.0 % to 85.1 % with augmenting the pH from 4.0 to 7.0, and then decreased from 84.5 % to 68.8 % when the pH values were further raised from 8.0 to 10.0. The results indicated that the catalytic activity of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI was highest at the pH of 7.0. The obvious reduction of the catalytic activity for the catalyst under acidic conditions may be originated from the stability of the catalyst. The skeleton of the catalyst is ZIF-8 MOFs, and it would be partially decomposed in acidic conditions [39,42], resulting in the significant decrease of the catalytic activity. Furthermore, the oxidation potential of hydroxyl radical (·OH) under acidic conditions would be higher than that in neutral environment, and the generation of ·OH in the catalytic system became difficult [63]. The Zeta potential of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI was determined to be + 35 mV, indicating the catalyst with a positive charge on its surface, while the target dye (AO) in acidic conditions was also a cation. Therefore, the electrostatic repulsion between the catalyst and the dye hindered the adsorption of AO on the active site of the catalyst (Fig. 5b), resulting in reduction of the degradation efficiency. In alkaline conditions, the hydroxyl ion (OH—) in the catalytic system may adsorb on the catalyst, which can cover some active sites and caused the decrease of the catalytic activity. The results showed that the neutral condition was more suitable for the degradation of AO dye.

The effect of the catalyst dosage on the degradation of AO (15.0 μg/mL) was investigated (Fig. 5c). The catalytic degradation percentages of AO for 40 min were 49.6 %, 86.5 %, 96.0 % and 94.6 % when the dosage of catalyst was 0.100 mg/mL, 0.300 mg/mL, 0.500 mg/mL and 0.700 mg/mL, respectively. The degradation efficiency of AO increased with augmenting the dosage of catalyst from 0.100 mg/mL to 0.500 mg/mL, and then remained constant, which indicated that the dosage of 0.500 mg/mL catalyst was enough to degrade the target dye.

The effect of co-existing ions on the catalytic performance of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI for degradation of AO has been investigated, and some cations and anions (Mg2+, Ca2+, NO3 –, Cl- and SO4 2-) were selected as interfering ions. It has been found that the anions, SO4 2- and NO3 –, didn’t seem to hinder the degradation of AO, while the anion Cl- can slightly reduce the catalytic activity of the catalyst (Fig. 5d). Generally, Cl- can be used as a scavenger of h+ and the generated Cl· has weak oxidation activity [7], thus lessening the catalytic activity of the catalyst. In the existence of metal ions (Mg2+ or Ca2+), catalytic degradation efficiency of AO was obviously reduced (Fig. 5d). The metal ions may form complexes with the target dye (AO), and then affected the interaction between the catalyst and AO [64] which can interfere the activity of the catalyst.

The reusability of the catalyst CDs@ZIF-8@AgI for photocatalytic degradation of AO under visible light was investigated (Fig.S7). After three cycles, the photocatalytic degradation percentage of AO can still reach approximately 80 %, which indicated that the catalyst has a certain reusability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic mechanism of the catalyst for degradation of AO
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the main active species in the photocatalytic degradation of AO, the effects of some capture reagents on the degradation efficiency were investigated (Fig. 6a). As shown in Fig. 6a, the degradation percentages of AO were decreased from 96.0 % to 21.4 % and 37.6 % in the existence of TEMPO and EDTA-2Na, respectively, suggesting that the super-oxide anion radical (·O2 –) and holes (h+) were the main active species and played a key role in the photocatalytic degradation reaction of AO [21]. With addition of IPA to the reaction system, the catalytic degradation percentages of AO were slightly reduced, indicating that the hydroxyl radical (·OH) was the minor active species [60].

Photoelectronic properties of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI have been determined and compared with those of ZIF-8@AgI, AgI, CDs@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8. Fig. 6b shows the transient photocurrent responses of all the materials versus time of light irradiation for eight cycles. It can be seen that the ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 had very weak photocurrent responses in every cycle, while the photocurrent density was significantly enhanced when AgI was deposited on the surface of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8, respectively. The high transient photocurrent response of the CDs@ZIF-8@AgI represented that it had high electron transfer ability and can effectively separate the photoelectron-hole pairs [41]. Meanwhile, the introduction of CDs and formation of AgI heterojunction had a synergistic effect to improve the photoelectric properties of the catalyst, which was consistent with its high photocatalytic activity for degradation of AO.

Fig. 6c shows the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI, ZIF-8@AgI, AgI, CDs@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8 particles. It can be seen from their Nyquist plots that the charge transfer resistance of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI was significantly lower than those of other materials [65], indicating that the internal surface charge migration rate of the catalyst was the fastest [66]. Therefore, introduction of CDs and AgI into the ZIF-8 based photocatalyst can achieve faster charge transfer from the surface to the active site of the catalyst, which can promote the catalytic degradation efficiency of the substrate [58].

According to the Kubelka-Munk theory, the relation of the optical band gap (Eg) and the absorption coefficient (α) of semiconductors is as following equation: αhν = A (hν − Eg)1/2, where h, ν and A are Planck’s constant, light frequency and proportional constant, respectively [63]. This equation can be converted to the following equation: (F(R)hν)2 = (αhν/A)2 = hν − Eg where F(R)=(1 − R)2/R, and R is the relative reflectivity of the semiconductors [67]. Fig. 6d shows the curves between the (F (R) hν)2 and hν for CDs@ZIF-8, AgI and CDs@ZIF-8@AgI, and their relative reflectivity (R) can be obtained from the UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (Fig. 1a). Extended the tangent lines of the curves to the horizontal axis and the intercept was the optical band gap of the materials. Therefore, the band gap values (Eg) of CDs@ZIF-8, AgI and CDs@ZIF-8@AgI were 2.78, 2.76 and 2.72 eV, respectively. It has been noted that the band gap narrowed after formation of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI heterojunction compared with those of CDs@ZIF-8 and AgI, indicating that the light absorption efficiency of the heterojunction enhanced [21].

The Mott-Schottky (M−S) curves were determined to estimate the flat band potential (Efb) of CDs@ZIF-8 and AgI (Fig. 6e and 6f). The positive slope observed from the M−S curves revealed the n-type semiconductor properties of AgI and CDs@ZIF-8 [45]. From Fig. 6e and 6f, the flat band potential (Efb) of CDs@ZIF-8 was −0.16 V vs Ag/AgCl and that of AgI was −0.69 V vs Ag/AgCl, which were obtained from the intercepts of the M−S curves [45]. Hence, corresponding Efb values of CDs@ZIF-8 and AgI were 0.04 V vs NHE and −0.49 V vs NHE, respectively [21]. The conduction band potential (CB) of n-type semiconductors was 0.1 V lower than the Efb [68,69], and so the CB (ECB) of CDs@ZIF-8 and AgI were −0.06 V vs NHE and −0.59 V vs NHE, respectively [21]. Therefore, the valence band potential (EVB) of CDs@ZIF-8-c and AgI can be calculated to be 2.72 V vs NHE and 2.17 V vs NHE, respectively [70].

The XRD pattern and UV–vis DRS spectrum of the recovered photocatalyst after the degradation reaction were collected and compared with those of the fresh CDs@ZIF-8@AgI (Fig.S8). A new weak peak appeared at 38.2° was observed in the XRD pattern of the recovered photocatalyst (Fig.S8a), which may be resulted from small amount of Ag0 (JCPDS:04–0783) formed in the process of photocatalysis [68]. Furthermore, UV–vis DRS spectrum of the recovered CDs@ZIF-8@AgI showed that the absorption intensity in the visible light region was obviously enhanced (Fig.S8b), which may be originated to the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of the metal Ag0 [71]. The results inferred that the Ag0 was formed on the surface of the photocatalyst under irradiation of visible light during the catalytic degradation process.

Based on the band structures and photoelectronic properties of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI, CDs@ZIF-8 and AgI, and the active species in the photocatalytic degradation reaction of AO, two possible photocatalytic electron transfer mechanisms were proposed, traditional type-II heterojunction and indirect Z-scheme heterojunction (Fig. 7 ). When CDs@ZIF-8@AgI composites are exposed to visible light, the electrons on VB of CDs@ZIF-8 and AgI were easily excited, and then the photoelectrons (e-) transferred to the CB of CDs@ZIF-8 and AgI, leaving holes (h+) on the VB. For the traditional type-II heterojunction mechanism (Fig. 7a), the e- on the CB (−0.59 V) of AgI can easily shift to the CB (−0.06 V) of CDs@ZIF-8, while the h+ on the VB of CDs@ZIF-8 (2.72 V) transferred to the VB of AgI (2.17 V) in the heterojunction of CDs@ZIF-8@AgI. Hence, the redox potential of the photogenerated carriers (e- and h+) was unavoidably reduced, and then, the electrons on the CB of CDs@ZIF-8 can’t react with the adsorbed oxygen to produce the O2 − radical because the reduction potential for generation of the O2 − radical was − 0.33 V [60], which was more negative than the CB of CDs@ZIF-8 (−0.06 V). Furthermore, the oxidation potential of OH radicals was 2.38 V [72], which was more positive than the VB of AgI (2.17 V), thus OH radicals could not be produced. Therefore, the type-II route was contradicted with the results of the active radical quenching experiments, in which the O2 − and OH radicals were demonstrated as the active species.

The indirect Z-scheme heterojunction type (Fig. 7b) can be more suitable to explain the photocatalytic mechanism. In this mechanism, the Ag0 species could be formed under irradiation of visible light on the surface of AgI, which can be used as an intermediate medium for receiving electrons and holes [20]. The electrons on the CB of CDs@ZIF-8 transferred to the Ag nanoparticles because the CB of CDs@ZIF-8 was more negative than the Fermi level of the Ag0 (EF= +0.4 V) [73]. Meanwhile, the holes on the VB of AgI shifted to the Fermi level of Ag0 since the VB of AgI was more positive than the EF of Ag0. Therefore, the photoelectrons in AgI with high reduction ability and holes in CDs@ZIF-8 with high oxidation ability can be maintained and the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons/holes can be enhanced. The electrons on the CB of AgI have enough reduction potential to reduce O2 to form O2 − radicals, while the holes on the VB of CDs@ZIF-8 have enough oxidation potential to directly oxidize the organic pollutant molecules or oxidize H2O to form OH. Thus, with the help of O2 –, h+ and OH, the AO molecules can be converted into degraded products. Possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism of AO was shown as following:
CDs@ZIF-8 + hv → CDs@ZIF-8 (e– + h+)
AgI + hv → AgI (e– + h+)
AgI (e–) + O2→ O2 −
CDs@ZIF-8 (h+) + H2O →OH
AO + O2 −, h+, OH → degraded products.

In addition, the degradation products of AO were analyzed by HPLC-Q-TOF-MS, and no component has been detected, and there was no new absorption band observed in the UV–vis spectra of the degradation products. Therefore, we proposed that the dye of AO may be degraded into small molecules of CO2 and H2O [71].

A visible light responsive photocatalyst, CDs@ZIF-8@AgI, has been successfully prepared by a solvent-free method coupled with in-situ ion deposition strategy. The catalytic degradation efficiency of AO dye can reach to 96 %, and the photocatalyst has the potential ability to degrade other organic dyes or antibiotics. The introduction of CDs into the heterojunction material can significantly improve the photocatalytic performance by enhancing the electron transport efficiency and visible light absorption capacity of the catalyst. The mechanism of photocatalytic degradation was proposed as the indirect Z-scheme heterojunction type. This work provides a heterojunction material for removal of AO dye in environmental wastewater under irradiation of visible light and offers a promising strategy for developing photocatalyst to treat the pollutants in the environment using solar energy.

Qianshu Xia: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yun Hao: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Suimin Deng: Investigation, Visualization. Lina Yang: Data curation. Ruoxin Wang: Investigation, Validation. Xiangfeng Wang: Methodology. Yuan Liu: Resources, Project administration. Hailing Liu: Methodology, Supervision. Mengxia Xie: Conceptualization, Resources, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.
